title: Machine Vision Lecture 4 Quiz: Morphological Processing
instructions: Answer all 30 multiple-choice questions. Select the best single answer for each item.

questions.prompt: Morphological processing is primarily concerned with:
options: Geometric structure and shape of binary objects.

questions.prompt: In a typical vision pipeline, morphology is most often applied:
options: Immediately after segmentation cleanup is needed.

questions.prompt: Lecture 4 uses which binary convention?
Why can examples showing 1 as black still be valid under the same morphology math?
options: Because morphology uses RGB values directly.
Because visual color rendering is irrelevant; membership in object/background sets matters.
Because black pixels are always background.
Because only anti-aliased images are supported.

questions.prompt: Which two primitive operations form the basis of classical morphology?
A structuring element (SE) in morphology is most analogous to:
The SE origin (anchor) is typically placed:
options: At the center of the SE.
At the brightest pixel in the image.

questions.prompt: Which is NOT a common SE shape discussed in Lecture 4?
An SE "fit" at position $(x,y)$ means:
options: At least one SE on-pixel overlaps object.
All SE on-pixels overlap object pixels.
SE overlaps exactly half object pixels.

questions.prompt: An SE "hit" at position $(x,y)$ means:
options: At least one SE on-pixel overlaps object.
All SE on-pixels must overlap object.
SE has odd width and height.

questions.prompt: An SE "miss" at position $(x,y)$ means:
options: SE overlaps object only at center.
SE overlaps object only at corners.
SE has no overlap with any object pixel.
SE does not fit but still hits.

questions.prompt: Erosion output at a pixel is 1 when:
options: The SE fits entirely within foreground.

questions.prompt: Dilation output at a pixel is 1 when:
options: The SE fits entirely within foreground.
The SE has no overlap with object.
The SE overlaps at least one foreground pixel.
The object is larger than the SE.

questions.prompt: What is the primary geometric effect of erosion?
options: It shrinks objects by removing boundary layers.

questions.prompt: What is the primary geometric effect of dilation?
options: It grows objects outward and can fill narrow breaks.
It removes all small holes without side effects.

questions.prompt: A practical benefit of erosion is that it can:
options: Separate objects connected by thin bridges.
Increase object area to original size.

questions.prompt: A practical benefit of dilation is that it can:
options: Bridge small gaps and repair narrow breaks.

questions.prompt: Why are erosion and dilation often not used alone?
options: They are undefined for binary images.
Each introduces size distortion when used by itself.

questions.prompt: A $20\times 30$ rectangle eroded by a $3\times 3$ square SE becomes approximately:
A $20\times 30$ rectangle dilated by a $3\times 3$ square SE becomes approximately:
Opening is defined as:
options: Dilation followed by erosion with same SE.
Erosion followed by dilation with same SE.

questions.prompt: Opening is especially useful for:
options: Filling small background holes inside objects.
Removing small foreground noise and thin protrusions.

questions.prompt: Closing is defined as:
options: Dilation followed by erosion with same SE.
Erosion followed by dilation with same SE.

questions.prompt: Closing is especially useful for:
options: Filling small holes and narrow gaps while preserving large object shape.

questions.prompt: Boundary extraction in Lecture 4 is given by:
For boundary extraction, increasing SE size generally makes the extracted boundary:
In iterative region filling $X_k = (X_{k-1} \oplus B) \cap A^c$, the intersection with $A^c$ mainly:
options: Acts as a containment mask so fill does not pass through boundary pixels.

questions.prompt: Region filling requires a closed boundary primarily because:
options: Otherwise the fill can leak outside the intended region.
The SE must always be a disc.
Seed points cannot be chosen in open curves.

questions.prompt: The iterative region-filling process terminates when:
options: The SE no longer hits the boundary.

questions.prompt: Which statement about higher-level morphological algorithms is correct?
options: Thinning reduces objects toward $1$-pixel-wide medial axes while preserving connectivity.
Connected components require grayscale gradients, not binary operations.
